commands.command: re-review — reshaped the mirror surface to **pin-from-here** to address the arbitrary-chat-id / authz concern: a thread opts itself in with `/pin on` and can only mirror the session it is already bound to (no arbitrary-target argument), so a pin never crosses a session boundary and adds zero new authorization surface. the operator `sessions echo` add paths now reject any target that is not a verified session participant (`targetmatchessessionparticipant`). also fixed the native streaming fan-out to use the turn's actual origin (`originatingchannel`) instead of stale `last*`, so a webchat/cross-channel trigger renders natively in the pinned channel rather than falling back to the flat post-hoc mirror
commands.freeform_prompt: — reshaped the mirror surface to **pin-from-here** to address the arbitrary-chat-id / authz concern: a thread opts itself in with `/pin on` and can only mirror the session it is already bound to (no arbitrary-target argument), so a pin never crosses a session boundary and adds zero new authorization surface. The operator `sessions echo` add paths now reject any target that is not a verified session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`). Also fixed the native streaming fan-out to use the turn's actual origin (`OriginatingChannel`) instead of stale `last*`, so a webchat/cross-channel trigger renders natively in the pinned channel rather than falling back to the flat post-hoc mirror.

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body:
## Channel echo / session pinning (pin-from-here) with native Telegram rendering

A session can mirror its turns to other threads that are bound to the **same**
session, so a long run you started in one place can be followed from another —
and the mirror renders **natively** in the destination channel (live draft
stream, not a flat `[echo]` copy).

### What changed since the earlier revision (maintainer feedback)

The earlier revision let a session pin **arbitrary** `{channel, to, threadId}`
echo targets. The maintainer's concern was that continuous mirroring to
arbitrary chat ids is a heavy primitive and lacks an authorization story for
streaming conversation content to any chat id. This revision reshapes the opt-in
surface to **pin-from-here**, which removes that surface entirely:

- A thread opts **itself** in with the in-chat command **`/pin on`** (alias
  `/mirror`). It can only mirror the session it is **already bound to** — there
  is no arbitrary-target argument — so a pin can never cross a session boundary.
  Every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant
  of that exact session. **Zero new authorization surface.**
- The operator add paths (`sessions echo` CLI + `sessions.echo` RPC) now **reject
  any target that is not a verified participant** of the session
  (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), with guidance to use `/pin` from the
  target thread instead. The "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path is gone.
- Self-exclusion is correct by construction: `/pin` captures the thread's own
  origin identity (the exact fields the fan-out uses as a turn's origin), so when
  this thread drives a turn it is excluded from its own fan-out; when a sibling
  thread of the same session drives, this thread receives the mirror.

`last*` (the session's persistent external delivery route) is unchanged — a
transient webchat/cron poke still must not hijack an external session's route
(session-delivery.ts, #47745).

### Native rendering for every origin

The native streaming renderer replays the origin run's agent-event bus into each
pinned channel's own compositor (one agent run, N native renders), so the mirror
carries the destination channel's real rendering rather than a flat final.

This revision also fixes a latent bug so the native path engages for **non-
channel-origin** turns (webchat `chat.send`, cron/heartbeat): the fan-out derived
its origin from the session's `last*`, which those turns deliberately do not
claim — so the origin defaulted to the previously-active channel and, when that
channel was itself the pinned target, the fan-out **self-excluded** it and fell
back to the flat post-hoc mirror. It now derives the fan-out origin from the
turn's **actual** origin (`OriginatingChannel/To`), falling back to `last*`, so a
webchat trigger renders natively in the pinned channel.

### New in this revision (3 commits on top of the existing channel-echo work)

- `feat(reply): add /pin (pin-from-here) in-chat command` — `commands-pin.ts` +
  registration; stores the caller's origin identity into the bound session's
  echo targets. Unit tests incl. an end-to-end check chaining `/pin` through the
  real `resolveEchoTargets` fan-out (sibling included, self excluded).
- `feat(echo): reject arbitrary-target echo adds; require a session participant`
  — `targetMatchesSessionParticipant` in `echo.ts`, wired into both operator add
  paths. Unit tests for accept/reject/fail-closed.
- `fix(echo): native streaming fan-out uses the turn's actual origin, not stale
  last*` — `agent-runner-execution.ts`.

The existing channel-echo infrastructure (native streaming fan-out, mirror
resolver, Telegram renderer, owner-bound `registerEchoRendererFactory`, the
types-only `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath) is unchanged by these three.

### Tests + CI (current head `d6c154cb22f`)

- `pnpm tsgo:core` + `pnpm tsgo:test:src`: 0 errors. `oxlint` on the changed
  files: 0 warnings / 0 errors.
- Unit tests for the changed surface pass, including the **152-test
  `agent-runner-execution.test`** suite the origin fix touches, plus
  `commands-pin.test`, `echo.test`, and `chat.send-user-echo.test`.

### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent)

Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow with a local patched
crabbox: a real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch (`OpenClaw 2026.5.31
d6c154c`, global-installed) + Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen
capture, run locally. A Telegram thread and webchat are bound to one session; the
thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session and the pinned thread
receives that webchat-origin turn rendered natively.

Captured sequence (real Telegram Desktop):
1. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."**
2. `📱 [via webchat] Please answer concisely.` — the webchat-origin prompt
3. The pinned thread receives the webchat-triggered reply.

![pin-from-here — webchat trigger mirrored to the pinned Telegram thread](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-telegram.png)

![motion](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-telegram-motion.gif)

Native-path engagement on the same build was confirmed by instrumenting the
fan-out: for the webchat-origin turn `resolveEchoTargets` returns the Telegram
target (origin = webchat, not self-excluded), the factory is found, and the
renderer is **created** (native). Gateway log for the filmed run:
`embedded run start … messageChannel=webchat` → `[telegram] outbound send ok
chatId=-1003900553563 messageId=194`. Session store after `/pin`:
`echoTargets: [{"channel":"telegram","to":"telegram:-1003900553563","threadId":1,"label":"pinned"}]`.

**Proof limitations:** the live run used a single-node local gateway with one
Telegram backend on an isolated state dir and a deterministic streaming mock
model (so the streamed text is reproducible); the mock emits plain text, so the
capture shows native delivery of the reply but not tool-call commentary. The
underlying echo pipeline exercised is the branch's real code.

### AI assistance

Built with AI assistance (Claude). Implementation, tests, and the local
behavior-proof capture were AI-assisted; framing and the proof are the author's.
target.author: anagnorisis2peripeteia
